explicit_text: On September 11, 2001, I was in the Ellis Island protection office filling out paperwork after returning from a wildland fire team in Utah. When the attacks began, Ellis Island was designated as a triage area for victims, and I helped gather medical supplies and set up for incoming patients. We received several boatloads of mostly walking wounded, and later the Jersey City Medical Center took over incident command there. Ellis Island also was considered as a temporary morgue site, though thankfully that was never needed. It became a hub of medical and logistical support during that chaotic day.
implicit_text: On September 11, 2001, I was in the immigration inspection office on the island near the Statue of Liberty filling out paperwork after returning from a wildland fire team in Utah. We received several boatloads of mostly walking wounded, and later the New Jersey Medical Center took over incident command there. This island also was considered as a temporary morgue site, though thankfully that was never needed. It became a hub of medical and logistical support during that chaotic day.

explicit_text: Before the events of September 11, I had spent 16 days in Utah on a wildland fire team, working on a security detail at a base camp. I had just returned from there and was in the Ellis Island protection office when the attacks started. My time in Utah was intense, and I was still in fire gear when I flew back, which caused some concern on the plane because I was carrying a Leatherman tool. That trip was fresh in my mind as the tragic events unfolded back in New York.

explicit_text: I first noticed smoke coming out of the Trade Center early in the morning before the park opened on September 11. Initially, we thought it might be a fire or some kind of smoke condition, or even a helicopter, since there are many helicopter tours in the area. But soon after, I saw a plane flying very low toward the Trade Center, and then the first impact happened. The explosion and fireball were shocking, and it was clear something terrible was unfolding at the Trade Center.

explicit_text: On the morning of September 11, while at Ellis Island, I saw the plane approaching very low across towards Governor's Island just before it hit the Trade Center. Governor's Island was in the line of sight as the plane came in, and I focused my camera on the plane from that vantage point. It was a surreal moment, witnessing the attack from near Governor's Island before the plane crashed into the building.

explicit_text: After the plane hit the Trade Center, I initially thought the plane might have passed through and landed somewhere near City Hall. That was my assumption because the flame appeared to come out the other side of the building, so I thought it continued traveling. Later, as events unfolded, it became clear that the situation was far worse than I had imagined, but City Hall was part of the area I was watching from Ellis Island that morning.

explicit_text: As the terrorist attacks unfolded on September 11, we received word that a plane had gone down in the Pentagon. This confirmed that the events were coordinated attacks on multiple targets. The news of the Pentagon crash added to the urgency and chaos, and airports nationwide were shut down with all airspace restricted. It was a sobering moment realizing the scale of the attacks beyond just New York City.

The news of the crash at the defense building added to the urgency and chaos, and airports nationwide were shut down with all airspace restricted. It was a sobering moment realizing the scale of the attacks beyond just New York City.

explicit_text: Jersey City played a key role during the September 11 attacks as the location of the New Jersey command center. Ellis Island was identified as a triage area connected to this command center, and later the Jersey City Medical Center arrived with a mobile command center to take over incident command. The coordination between Ellis Island and Jersey City was vital in managing the medical response that day.

The coordination between the island and this city was vital in managing the medical response that day.

explicit_text: On September 11, I went over to Liberty Island with another EMT to get additional medical supplies, which was unsettling because we were all nervous about something happening to the Statue of Liberty. The EMS room there was next to the communications center, and while we were there, we heard the first tower collapse on the radio and saw it on video. Liberty Island was a critical support location during the attacks, and I was relieved to see the statue still standing despite the chaos.

explicit_text: The World Trade Center was the epicenter of the attacks on September 11, and I witnessed the first plane hit and the subsequent collapse of the towers from nearby Ellis Island and Liberty Island. Later, I attempted to dock near the World Trade Center at North Cove Marina with emergency responders but found no room due to the many police and fire boats. The devastation at the World Trade Center was overwhelming, and it shaped the entire day's response efforts.

explicit_text: On the day of the attacks, we tried to dock at North Cove Marina near the World Trade Center because it was where all the emergency boats were gathered. However, there was no room to dock due to the heavy presence of police and fireboats. We had to return to our main docking area at the Marine Inspection Office in Battery Park instead. North Cove Marina was a focal point for emergency response boats during the crisis.

explicit_text: Battery Park was the location of the Marine Inspection Office, which served as our main docking area after we couldn't dock at North Cove Marina. When we returned there, I saw my colleague Charlie Deleo standing at the dock covered in soot and debris. We also headed into the Battery Park area to see if we could assist people coming out of Lower Manhattan. Battery Park was a key access point during the emergency response on September 11.

explicit_text: Charlie Deleo was a longtime park volunteer known as the Keeper of the Flame because he took care of the Statue of Liberty's torch. On September 11, I saw him standing at the dock at Battery Park covered in soot and debris, just watching the statue. Despite the chaos, Charlie stayed there for a long time, deeply connected to the statue and the events unfolding around us. It was a poignant moment seeing someone so dedicated in the midst of such tragedy.

explicit_text: Lower Manhattan was the area most directly affected by the attacks, and many people were evacuated from there. We tried to assist by docking near the World Trade Center but had to return to Battery Park due to crowded emergency boats. Ambulance companies told us there were no more patients to bring out, just people needing to get out of Lower Manhattan. The area was chaotic, and our role was mostly to help those walking wounded and provide support after the initial rescues.

explicit_text: A few days after the attacks, I went into Ground Zero to help with rescue efforts despite my wife's concerns. On the first day, I stayed mostly on Ellis Island and nearby locations, but the devastation at Ground Zero was the focus of all rescue and recovery work. Visiting Ground Zero was intense and emotional, as it was the heart of the disaster site where the towers once stood.

explicit_text: On September 11, 2001, I was at Ellis Island when the attacks began, witnessing the first plane hit the Trade Center and the subsequent collapse of the towers. The day unfolded rapidly with evacuations, medical triage setup, and coordination with emergency responders from Jersey City and beyond. I helped gather medical supplies, assisted with triage, and even went into Lower Manhattan to offer support. It was a day of shock, disbelief, and intense response to an unprecedented terrorist attack.

explicit_text: Just days before September 11, while flying back from Utah in my fire gear, I had a Leatherman tool on my belt. A fellow passenger complained about me carrying a knife, but the stewardess explained it was just a Leatherman. It was ironic because after the attacks, security tightened so much that even small items like toothpicks were banned on planes. That moment stuck with me as a small but telling detail of how much changed after the attacks.

explicit_text: The Jersey City Medical Center played a crucial role on September 11 by bringing a mobile command center to Ellis Island and taking over incident command of the medical response. Dozens of EMTs, paramedics, and emergency room physicians arrived under their coordination. Their presence helped organize the triage and treatment of patients arriving by boat from Lower Manhattan. They were a vital part of the emergency medical infrastructure that day.

It provided a base for coordinating medical and rescue efforts coming from the water.

explicit_text: Liberty State Park was mentioned as a possible location where bodies might be brought instead of Ellis Island, which was initially set up as a temporary morgue but lacked refrigeration. Although we never received any deceased at Ellis Island, it seems that Liberty State Park was used for that purpose or at least considered for it. It was part of the broader regional response to the attacks, supporting the handling of victims and logistics.
